---
title: "Build Your Own Coding Agent"
subtitle: "A Practical Guide to Open-Source AI Coding Assistants"
author: "Michele Chini"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: github
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
execute:
  enabled: false
---

# Introduction

## What is a Coding Agent?

A **coding agent** is an AI system that can autonomously write, edit, debug, and reason about code. Unlike a simple chatbot that answers programming questions, a coding agent can:

- Read and navigate your codebase
- Execute shell commands and interpret results
- Edit files across your project
- Run tests and fix failures iteratively
- Plan multi-step tasks and self-correct

You've probably heard of tools like **Claude Code** (Anthropic), **GitHub Copilot** (Microsoft), or **Codex CLI** (OpenAI). These are commercial, closed-source agents. But there's a growing ecosystem of **open-source alternatives** that you can run locally, customize, and integrate into your own workflows — without sending proprietary code to third-party servers.

## Why Should You Care?

If you work in a regulated industry (banking, healthcare, government), sending source code to external APIs may violate compliance policies. Open-source coding agents let you:

- **Keep code on-premise**: Run models locally or on your own infrastructure
- **Customize behaviour**: Add domain-specific tools, enforce coding standards, integrate with internal systems
- **Control costs**: No per-token API fees for local models
- **Audit everything**: Full transparency into what the model sees and does

## What You'll Learn

By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

1. Understand the architecture of a coding agent
2. Choose the right open-source model using benchmarks
3. Set up a local coding agent with working code
4. Extend it with custom tools
5. (Bonus) Use API-based models when local isn't enough

---

# The Landscape: Open-Source Coding Agents

Before building our own, let's map what already exists.

| Tool | Description | Model Support | License |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------|
| [Aider](https://aider.chat) | Terminal-based pair programmer | Any LLM (local or API) | Apache 2.0 |
| [Open Interpreter](https://openinterpreter.com) | Natural language interface to your computer | Any LLM | AGPL 3.0 |
| [SWE-agent](https://swe-agent.com) | Autonomous bug-fixing agent | Any LLM | MIT |
| [Continue](https://continue.dev) | IDE extension (VS Code / JetBrains) | Any LLM | Apache 2.0 |
| [Tabby](https://tabby.tabbyml.com) | Self-hosted code completion | Open models | Apache 2.0 |
| [Goose](https://github.com/block/goose) | Autonomous coding agent by Block | Any LLM | Apache 2.0 |
| [OpenCode](https://opencode.ai) | Terminal coding agent (like Claude Code, but open-source) | Any LLM | MIT |

::: {.callout-tip}
## Quick Start Recommendation
If you want to try an open-source coding agent **right now** without building anything, install [Aider](https://aider.chat). It works with local models via Ollama and takes 5 minutes to set up.
:::

---

# Architecture of a Coding Agent

Every coding agent, whether commercial or open-source, shares the same core architecture:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[User Prompt] --> B[LLM Brain]
    B --> C{Tool Selection}
    C --> D[Read Files]
    C --> E[Edit Files]
    C --> F[Run Commands]
    C --> G[Search Code]
    D --> B
    E --> B
    F --> B
    G --> B
    B --> H[Response to User]
```

## The Four Components

### 1. The LLM (the "Brain")

The large language model that reasons about code. It receives context (your files, your request) and decides what to do next.

### 2. Tools (the "Hands")

Functions the LLM can call to interact with the outside world:

- **File operations**: read, write, edit files
- **Shell execution**: run commands (tests, builds, git)
- **Search**: find files, grep for patterns
- **Web access**: fetch documentation, search for solutions

### 3. The Agent Loop

The orchestration logic that:

1. Sends the user's request + context to the LLM
2. Parses the LLM's response for tool calls
3. Executes the tools
4. Feeds results back to the LLM
5. Repeats until the task is complete

### 4. Context Management

Code projects can be huge. The agent needs strategies to:

- Select only relevant files to include in the prompt
- Summarize large files
- Maintain conversation history without exceeding token limits

---

# Choosing the Right Model

## Where to Find Models

The go-to resource is [Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co), the largest open-source ML platform. Here's how to navigate it:

### Hugging Face Model Hub

Go to [huggingface.co/models](https://huggingface.co/models) and filter by:

- **Task**: `Text Generation`
- **Library**: `transformers`, `gguf` (for local inference)
- **Sort by**: `Trending` or `Most Downloads`

### What to Look For in a Model Card

Every model on Hugging Face has a **model card** — a README with key information. Here's what matters for coding agents:

| Field | What to check |
|-------|--------------|
| **Parameters** | 7B runs on a laptop, 13B-34B needs a good GPU, 70B+ needs multi-GPU |
| **Context window** | Minimum 8K tokens, ideally 32K+ for code |
| **License** | `Apache 2.0` or `MIT` for commercial use. Watch out for `Llama Community License` restrictions |
| **Training data** | Was it trained on code? Look for mentions of GitHub, The Stack, CodeSearchNet |
| **Quantization** | GGUF/GPTQ versions available? These run faster with less RAM |

### Example: Evaluating a Model

Let's say you're considering `Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct`. On its Hugging Face page you'd check:

```{python}
#| label: check-model-info
# You can inspect model metadata programmatically using the huggingface_hub library

# pip install huggingface_hub

from huggingface_hub import model_info

model = model_info("Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct")

print(f"Model:       {model.modelId}")
print(f"Downloads:   {model.downloads:,}")
print(f"Likes:       {model.likes:,}")
print(f"License:     {model.card_data.license if model.card_data else 'N/A'}")
print(f"Tags:        {model.tags}")
print(f"Pipeline:    {model.pipeline_tag}")
print(f"Library:     {model.library_name}")
```

## Key Benchmarks for Coding Models

Not all benchmarks are equal. Here are the ones that actually matter for coding agents:

### SWE-bench

The gold standard for evaluating coding agents. It tests whether an agent can autonomously resolve real GitHub issues from popular Python repositories.

- **What it measures**: End-to-end bug fixing (read issue → find code → write patch → pass tests)
- **Where to check**: [swebench.com](https://www.swebench.com)
- **What's a good score**: Top agents score 40-50% on SWE-bench Verified

### BigCodeBench

Evaluates code generation across 1,140 practical programming tasks.

- **What it measures**: Function-level code generation with real-world library usage
- **Where to check**: [bigcode-bench.github.io](https://bigcode-bench.github.io)

### HumanEval / HumanEval+

The classic benchmark: 164 hand-written Python programming problems.

- **What it measures**: Basic code generation ability
- **Limitation**: Too easy for modern models — most score 80%+
- **Where to check**: [evalplus.github.io](https://evalplus.github.io/leaderboard.html)

### LiveCodeBench

Fresh competitive programming problems (post-training-cutoff) to avoid data contamination.

- **What it measures**: Genuine reasoning and problem-solving
- **Where to check**: [livecodebench.github.io](https://livecodebench.github.io)

### How to Compare Models on Hugging Face

```{python}
#| label: search-coding-models
# Search for coding models and compare them

from huggingface_hub import list_models

# Search for coding-focused models, sorted by downloads
coding_models = list_models(
    search="coder",
    sort="downloads",
    direction=-1,
    limit=10,
)

print(f"{'Model':<50} {'Downloads':>12} {'Likes':>8}")
print("-" * 72)
for m in coding_models:
    print(f"{m.modelId:<50} {m.downloads:>12,} {m.likes:>8,}")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Recommended Open-Weight Models for Coding (as of early 2026)

| Model | Params | Context | Strengths |
|-------|--------|---------|-----------|
| Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct | 32B | 128K | Best overall open coding model |
| DeepSeek-Coder-V2 | 236B (MoE) | 128K | Massive capability, needs big hardware |
| CodeLlama-70B-Instruct | 70B | 16K | Meta's best coding model |
| StarCoder2-15B | 15B | 16K | Permissive license, good for fine-tuning |
| DeepSeek-R1-0528 | 671B (MoE) | 128K | State-of-the-art reasoning |
:::

---

# Setting Up Your Environment

## Prerequisites

You need:

- **Python 3.10+**
- **A local LLM runtime** (we'll use Ollama — it's the simplest)
- **Basic terminal skills** (you know what `cd`, `pip install`, and `git` do)

Hardware requirements depend on model size:

| Model size | RAM needed | GPU VRAM | Example hardware |
|-----------|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| 7B (Q4) | 8 GB | 6 GB | Laptop with decent GPU |
| 13B (Q4) | 16 GB | 10 GB | Gaming desktop |
| 32B (Q4) | 32 GB | 24 GB | RTX 4090 / Mac M2 Pro |
| 70B (Q4) | 64 GB | 48 GB | 2x RTX 4090 / Mac M2 Ultra |

## Option A: Ollama (Command-Line)

[Ollama](https://ollama.com) lets you run open-weight models locally with a single command. It's lightweight, fast, and ideal if you're comfortable with the terminal.

### Install Ollama

```{bash}
#| label: install-ollama
# macOS / Linux
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Or on macOS via Homebrew:
# brew install ollama
```

Verify it's working:

```{bash}
#| label: verify-ollama
ollama --version
```

### Pull a Coding Model

```{bash}
#| label: pull-model
# Pull Qwen2.5-Coder (7B is a good starting point)
ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:7b

# For a more capable model (needs 24GB+ RAM):
# ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:32b
```

Test it with a quick prompt:

```{bash}
#| label: test-model
ollama run qwen2.5-coder:7b "Write a Python function to check if a number is prime"
```

### Ollama's API

Ollama exposes an **OpenAI-compatible API** on `http://localhost:11434`. This means any code written for the OpenAI API works with Ollama — just change the base URL. We'll use this extensively in the hands-on sections.

## Option B: LM Studio (GUI) {#sec-lmstudio}

[LM Studio](https://lmstudio.ai) is a **desktop application** for running local models. If you prefer a graphical interface over the terminal, this is for you.

### Why LM Studio?

- **Visual model browser**: Search, filter, and download models from Hugging Face directly in the app — no command line needed
- **One-click download**: Pick a model, click download, and it handles quantization format selection (GGUF) automatically
- **Built-in chat UI**: Test models in a ChatGPT-like interface before using them programmatically
- **Performance dashboard**: See tokens/second, memory usage, and GPU utilization in real time
- **OpenAI-compatible server**: Like Ollama, LM Studio can serve models via a local API

### Install LM Studio

1. Download from [lmstudio.ai](https://lmstudio.ai) (available for macOS, Windows, Linux)
2. Install and open the application
3. You'll see the **Discover** tab — this is the model browser

### Downloading a Model

In the **Discover** tab:

1. Search for `qwen2.5-coder` (or any model you want)
2. You'll see various quantization options (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q8_0, etc.)
   - **Q4_K_M**: Good balance of quality and size (recommended for most users)
   - **Q8_0**: Higher quality, needs more RAM
   - **FP16**: Full precision, needs significantly more RAM/VRAM
3. Click the download button next to your chosen variant
4. Wait for the download to complete

::: {.callout-tip}
## Choosing Quantization in LM Studio
LM Studio shows you the file size and estimated RAM usage for each quantization variant. A good rule of thumb: pick the largest quantization that fits in your available RAM with ~2GB headroom.
:::

### Using the Chat Interface

Once a model is downloaded:

1. Go to the **Chat** tab (left sidebar)
2. Select your model from the dropdown at the top
3. Start chatting — use this to quickly test if a model is good at coding before integrating it into your workflow

### Running LM Studio as a Local Server

This is the key feature for integration with coding agents:

1. Go to the **Developer** tab (left sidebar, `<>` icon)
2. Select a model to serve
3. Click **Start Server**
4. The server runs at `http://localhost:1234` by default

Now you can point any OpenAI-compatible tool at it:

```{python}
#| label: lmstudio-client
from openai import OpenAI

# LM Studio uses port 1234 by default (Ollama uses 11434)
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:1234/v1",
    api_key="lm-studio",  # LM Studio doesn't require a real key
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="qwen2.5-coder-7b",  # The model name shown in LM Studio
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Write a Python function to reverse a linked list"}
    ],
    temperature=0.1,
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

### LM Studio vs Ollama

| Feature | Ollama | LM Studio |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| Interface | CLI only | GUI + CLI |
| Model discovery | `ollama list` / website | Built-in browser with filters |
| Ease of use | Easy (for terminal users) | Easiest (point and click) |
| API port | `localhost:11434` | `localhost:1234` |
| Resource monitoring | Limited | Built-in dashboard |
| Scriptability | Excellent | Good (via API) |
| Multi-model serving | One at a time | One at a time |
| Platform | macOS, Linux, Windows | macOS, Linux, Windows |

::: {.callout-note}
## They're Interchangeable
Both Ollama and LM Studio expose OpenAI-compatible APIs. All the code in this guide works with either — just swap the `base_url`:

- Ollama: `http://localhost:11434/v1`
- LM Studio: `http://localhost:1234/v1`
:::

## Install Python Dependencies

```{bash}
#| label: install-deps
pip install openai huggingface_hub rich
```

---

# Building a Minimal Coding Agent

Let's build a coding agent from scratch. We'll start simple and add features incrementally.

## Version 1: A Chat Loop with Tool Calling

This is the simplest possible agent: it can read files, list directories, and run shell commands.

```{python}
#| label: minimal-agent
#| code-fold: false
"""
minimal_agent.py — A bare-bones coding agent using a local LLM via Ollama.
"""

import json
import subprocess
import os
from openai import OpenAI

# ── Configuration ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MODEL = "qwen2.5-coder:7b"  # Change to your preferred model
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:11434/v1",  # Ollama's OpenAI-compatible endpoint
    api_key="ollama",                       # Ollama doesn't need a real key
)

# ── Tool Definitions ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# These follow the OpenAI function-calling schema.
# The LLM reads these descriptions to decide when and how to call each tool.

TOOLS = [
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "read_file",
            "description": "Read the contents of a file at the given path.",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "path": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "Absolute or relative file path"
                    }
                },
                "required": ["path"]
            }
        }
    },
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "list_directory",
            "description": "List files and folders in a directory.",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "path": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "Directory path (default: current directory)"
                    }
                },
                "required": []
            }
        }
    },
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "run_command",
            "description": "Execute a shell command and return its output. Use for running tests, git commands, etc.",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "command": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "The shell command to execute"
                    }
                },
                "required": ["command"]
            }
        }
    },
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "write_file",
            "description": "Write content to a file. Creates the file if it doesn't exist, overwrites if it does.",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "path": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "File path to write to"
                    },
                    "content": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "Content to write"
                    }
                },
                "required": ["path", "content"]
            }
        }
    },
]


# ── Tool Implementations ─────────────────────────────────────────────

def read_file(path: str) -> str:
    """Read a file and return its contents (with basic safety check)."""
    path = os.path.abspath(path)
    if not os.path.isfile(path):
        return f"Error: File not found: {path}"
    try:
        with open(path, "r") as f:
            content = f.read()
        # Truncate very large files to avoid blowing up the context
        if len(content) > 10_000:
            return content[:10_000] + f"\n\n... [truncated, file is {len(content)} chars]"
        return content
    except Exception as e:
        return f"Error reading file: {e}"


def list_directory(path: str = ".") -> str:
    """List directory contents."""
    path = path or "."
    try:
        entries = os.listdir(path)
        entries.sort()
        result = []
        for entry in entries:
            full = os.path.join(path, entry)
            marker = "/" if os.path.isdir(full) else ""
            result.append(f"  {entry}{marker}")
        return f"Contents of {os.path.abspath(path)}:\n" + "\n".join(result)
    except Exception as e:
        return f"Error: {e}"


def run_command(command: str) -> str:
    """Execute a shell command with a timeout."""
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            command,
            shell=True,
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
            timeout=30,  # Safety: don't let commands run forever
        )
        output = result.stdout + result.stderr
        if len(output) > 5_000:
            output = output[:5_000] + "\n... [truncated]"
        return output or "(no output)"
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        return "Error: Command timed out after 30 seconds"
    except Exception as e:
        return f"Error: {e}"


def write_file(path: str, content: str) -> str:
    """Write content to a file."""
    try:
        os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(path) or ".", exist_ok=True)
        with open(path, "w") as f:
            f.write(content)
        return f"Successfully wrote {len(content)} chars to {path}"
    except Exception as e:
        return f"Error writing file: {e}"


# Map tool names to their implementations
TOOL_MAP = {
    "read_file": read_file,
    "list_directory": list_directory,
    "run_command": run_command,
    "write_file": write_file,
}


# ── Agent Loop ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a helpful coding assistant with access to the local filesystem.
You can read files, list directories, write files, and run shell commands.
Always read relevant files before suggesting changes. Think step by step.
When editing code, show the complete modified file."""


def run_agent():
    """Main agent loop: chat with the user, call tools as needed."""
    messages = [{"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT}]
    print("Coding Agent (type 'quit' to exit)")
    print("=" * 40)

    while True:
        # Get user input
        user_input = input("\nYou: ").strip()
        if user_input.lower() in ("quit", "exit", "q"):
            print("Goodbye!")
            break
        if not user_input:
            continue

        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_input})

        # Agent loop: keep going until the LLM responds without tool calls
        while True:
            response = client.chat.completions.create(
                model=MODEL,
                messages=messages,
                tools=TOOLS,
                temperature=0.1,
            )

            choice = response.choices[0]
            message = choice.message

            # If the model wants to call tools, execute them
            if message.tool_calls:
                # Add the assistant's message (with tool calls) to history
                messages.append(message)

                for tool_call in message.tool_calls:
                    fn_name = tool_call.function.name
                    fn_args = json.loads(tool_call.function.arguments)

                    print(f"  [Tool: {fn_name}({fn_args})]")

                    # Execute the tool
                    if fn_name in TOOL_MAP:
                        result = TOOL_MAP[fn_name](**fn_args)
                    else:
                        result = f"Error: Unknown tool '{fn_name}'"

                    # Add tool result to message history
                    messages.append({
                        "role": "tool",
                        "tool_call_id": tool_call.id,
                        "content": result,
                    })

                # Continue the loop — the LLM will see the tool results
                continue

            else:
                # No tool calls — the LLM is done, print its response
                print(f"\nAgent: {message.content}")
                messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": message.content})
                break


if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_agent()
```

### How It Works

1. The user types a request (e.g., "read main.py and add error handling")
2. The LLM receives the request + tool definitions
3. The LLM decides to call `read_file("main.py")`
4. The agent executes the tool and feeds the file contents back to the LLM
5. The LLM now has the file contents and can reason about changes
6. The LLM calls `write_file("main.py", "...")` with the updated code
7. The agent writes the file and confirms
8. The LLM responds to the user that the task is done

### Try It

```{bash}
#| label: run-agent
# Make sure Ollama is running first:
# ollama serve

python minimal_agent.py
```

---

# Adding Intelligence: Better Context and Search

The minimal agent works, but it's naive — it has no way to search a codebase. Let's add **grep** and **glob** tools, plus smarter context management.

## Version 2: With Code Search

```{python}
#| label: search-tools
"""
Additional tools to make the agent smarter at navigating code.
Add these to the TOOLS list and TOOL_MAP from Version 1.
"""

import glob as glob_module


def search_files(pattern: str, path: str = ".") -> str:
    """Search for files matching a glob pattern."""
    try:
        matches = glob_module.glob(pattern, root_dir=path, recursive=True)
        matches.sort()
        if not matches:
            return f"No files matching '{pattern}'"
        result = matches[:50]  # Limit results
        output = f"Files matching '{pattern}':\n"
        output += "\n".join(f"  {m}" for m in result)
        if len(matches) > 50:
            output += f"\n  ... and {len(matches) - 50} more"
        return output
    except Exception as e:
        return f"Error: {e}"


def grep_code(pattern: str, path: str = ".", file_type: str = "") -> str:
    """Search file contents using grep."""
    cmd = f'grep -rn "{pattern}" "{path}"'
    if file_type:
        cmd += f' --include="*.{file_type}"'
    cmd += " | head -30"  # Limit output
    return run_command(cmd)


# Add to TOOLS list:
SEARCH_TOOLS = [
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "search_files",
            "description": "Find files matching a glob pattern (e.g., '**/*.py' for all Python files).",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "pattern": {"type": "string", "description": "Glob pattern"},
                    "path": {"type": "string", "description": "Root directory (default: '.')"}
                },
                "required": ["pattern"]
            }
        }
    },
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "grep_code",
            "description": "Search for a text pattern in file contents. Returns matching lines with file paths and line numbers.",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "pattern": {"type": "string", "description": "Search pattern (regex supported)"},
                    "path": {"type": "string", "description": "Directory to search in"},
                    "file_type": {"type": "string", "description": "File extension filter (e.g., 'py', 'js')"}
                },
                "required": ["pattern"]
            }
        }
    },
]
```

---

# Using Aider: The Fastest Path to a Working Agent

If building from scratch feels like too much, [Aider](https://aider.chat) is a production-ready open-source coding agent that works with local models out of the box.

## Install and Run

```{bash}
#| label: install-aider
pip install aider-chat
```

## Use with a Local Model (via Ollama)

```{bash}
#| label: aider-ollama
# Start aider with a local model
aider --model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:7b

# For a better experience with a bigger model:
# aider --model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b
```

## Use with DeepSeek API (cheap and powerful)

```{bash}
#| label: aider-deepseek
# DeepSeek offers very cheap API access to their coding model
export DEEPSEEK_API_KEY="your-key-here"
aider --model deepseek/deepseek-coder
```

## Key Aider Commands

Once inside Aider:

```
/add file.py          # Add a file to the chat context
/drop file.py         # Remove a file from context
/run pytest           # Run a command
/diff                 # Show pending changes
/undo                 # Revert last change
/help                 # Show all commands
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Aider + Git
Aider automatically creates git commits for every change it makes. This means you can always `git diff` or `git revert` if something goes wrong. Very safe for experimentation.
:::

---

# Using SWE-agent: Autonomous Bug Fixing

[SWE-agent](https://swe-agent.com) is designed specifically for resolving GitHub issues autonomously. It's what researchers use to evaluate models on SWE-bench.

## Install

```{bash}
#| label: install-sweagent
pip install sweagent
```

## Run on a GitHub Issue

```{bash}
#| label: sweagent-run
# Resolve a GitHub issue using a local model
sweagent run \
  --agent.model.name ollama:qwen2.5-coder:7b \
  --problem_statement.github_url https://github.com/user/repo/issues/42
```

## Run on a Local Problem

```{bash}
#| label: sweagent-local
# Or describe the problem directly
sweagent run \
  --agent.model.name ollama:qwen2.5-coder:7b \
  --problem_statement.text "Fix the off-by-one error in utils/parser.py line 42"
```

---

# Using OpenCode: The Open-Source Claude Code {#sec-opencode}

[OpenCode](https://opencode.ai) is a terminal-based coding agent that feels very similar to **Claude Code** or **Codex CLI** — but it's fully open-source (MIT licensed) and works with any LLM provider, including local models.

If you've used Claude Code and want something similar that works with open-weight models or your own API keys, OpenCode is the closest match.

## What Makes OpenCode Different?

| Feature | OpenCode | Aider | SWE-agent |
|---------|----------|-------|-----------|
| **UX** | Rich terminal UI (like Claude Code) | Simple chat | Autonomous (no interaction) |
| **Interaction style** | Interactive, iterative | Interactive | Fully autonomous |
| **File editing** | Inline diffs with approval | Git-based patches | Patch files |
| **LSP support** | Yes (diagnostics, go-to-definition) | No | No |
| **Session management** | Built-in (resume previous sessions) | Git history | Logs |
| **MCP support** | Yes | No | No |

## Install OpenCode

```{bash}
#| label: install-opencode
# macOS / Linux — using the install script
curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install | bash

# Or via Go (if you have Go installed)
go install github.com/opencode-ai/opencode@latest
```

Verify the installation:

```{bash}
#| label: verify-opencode
opencode --version
```

## Configuration

OpenCode uses a configuration file (`opencode.json`) in your project root. Create one to set up your preferred model:

### With Ollama (local)

```json
{
  "provider": {
    "ollama": {
      "models": {
        "qwen-coder": {
          "model": "qwen2.5-coder:7b",
          "api_url": "http://localhost:11434/v1"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "model": "qwen-coder"
}
```

### With LM Studio (local)

```json
{
  "provider": {
    "lmstudio": {
      "models": {
        "coder": {
          "model": "qwen2.5-coder-7b",
          "api_url": "http://localhost:1234/v1"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "model": "coder"
}
```

### With an API Provider

```json
{
  "provider": {
    "anthropic": {
      "api_key": "your-key-here"
    }
  },
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6-20250514"
}
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Environment Variables
Instead of putting API keys in the config file, set them as environment variables. OpenCode automatically reads `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, and `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`.
:::

## Using OpenCode

### Basic Usage

```{bash}
#| label: opencode-usage
# Start OpenCode in your project directory
cd my-project
opencode
```

This opens an interactive terminal UI. You can:

- Type natural language requests ("add input validation to the login function")
- See file diffs before they're applied
- Approve or reject changes
- Ask follow-up questions

### Non-Interactive Mode

```{bash}
#| label: opencode-noninteractive
# Run a single task and exit
opencode -p "refactor the database connection to use connection pooling"
```

### Key Features in Practice

**File context**: OpenCode automatically indexes your project and pulls in relevant files based on your query — you don't need to manually add files like in Aider.

**Diff review**: When OpenCode proposes a change, you see a side-by-side diff and can accept, reject, or ask for modifications. This is similar to Claude Code's permission model.

**Session persistence**: If you close OpenCode and reopen it, you can resume your previous session with full context.

**LSP integration**: OpenCode can connect to language servers for real-time diagnostics. If your change introduces a type error or syntax issue, it catches it immediately.

## OpenCode vs Claude Code vs Codex CLI

| Feature | OpenCode | Claude Code | Codex CLI |
|---------|----------|-------------|-----------|
| Open source | Yes (MIT) | No | Partially |
| Model choice | Any (local or API) | Claude only | OpenAI only |
| Cost | Free + model costs | Anthropic pricing | OpenAI pricing |
| Terminal UI | Rich TUI | Rich TUI | Rich TUI |
| Tool use | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| On-premise | Yes | No | No |
| MCP support | Yes | Yes | No |

::: {.callout-note}
## When to Choose OpenCode
OpenCode is ideal if you want a **Claude Code-like experience** but need to (a) use open-weight models for privacy/compliance, (b) avoid vendor lock-in, or (c) customize the agent's behaviour. It's particularly well-suited for teams in regulated industries who can't send code to external APIs.
:::

---

# Brief Guide to API-Based Models {#sec-api-models}

Sometimes local models aren't enough — you need the full power of frontier models. Here's a quick overview.

## When to Use API Models

| Scenario | Recommendation |
|----------|---------------|
| Simple code generation, autocomplete | Local model (7B-32B) |
| Complex refactoring, multi-file changes | API model or large local (70B+) |
| Regulated environment, no external access | Local model only |
| Prototyping, exploring ideas | API model (faster iteration) |

## Provider Comparison

| Provider | Model | Pricing (approx.) | Strengths |
|----------|-------|-------------------|-----------|
| Anthropic | Claude Sonnet 4.6 | $3 / $15 per 1M tokens | Best for careful, thorough coding |
| OpenAI | GPT-4.1 | $2 / $8 per 1M tokens | Good all-rounder |
| DeepSeek | DeepSeek-R1 | $0.55 / $2.19 per 1M tokens | Excellent value, strong reasoning |
| Google | Gemini 2.5 Pro | $1.25-2.50 / $10-15 per 1M tokens | Huge context window (1M tokens) |

*Prices: input / output per million tokens. Check provider websites for current pricing.*

## Example: Using Claude API with Our Agent

To switch our minimal agent from a local model to Claude, just change the client configuration:

```{python}
#| label: claude-api-example
"""
Swap the OpenAI client for Anthropic's API.
Everything else (tools, agent loop) stays the same!
"""

# pip install anthropic

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()  # Uses ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env variable

# The Anthropic SDK uses a slightly different format for tool calling,
# but the concept is identical. Here's the key difference:

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6-20250514",
    max_tokens=4096,
    system="You are a helpful coding assistant.",
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Read main.py and explain what it does"}
    ],
    tools=[
        {
            "name": "read_file",
            "description": "Read a file's contents",
            "input_schema": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "path": {"type": "string", "description": "File path"}
                },
                "required": ["path"]
            }
        }
    ]
)

# Handle tool use in the response
for block in response.content:
    if block.type == "tool_use":
        print(f"Model wants to call: {block.name}({block.input})")
    elif block.type == "text":
        print(block.text)
```

## Example: Using OpenAI-Compatible APIs

Many providers (DeepSeek, Together, Groq, Fireworks) offer OpenAI-compatible endpoints. Our minimal agent works with all of them — just change the base URL:

```{python}
#| label: openai-compatible
from openai import OpenAI

# DeepSeek
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="https://api.deepseek.com",
    api_key="your-deepseek-key",
)

# Together AI (hosts many open models)
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="https://api.together.xyz/v1",
    api_key="your-together-key",
)

# Groq (extremely fast inference)
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
    api_key="your-groq-key",
)

# The rest of the agent code is identical!
```

---

# Security Considerations

When building a coding agent — especially one that can execute shell commands — security matters.

## Key Principles

1. **Sandboxing**: Never run an agent with root privileges. Use Docker containers or restricted user accounts.

2. **Path restrictions**: Limit file access to the project directory:

```{python}
#| label: path-safety
import os

ALLOWED_ROOT = os.path.abspath("./my-project")

def safe_read_file(path: str) -> str:
    """Read a file, but only within the allowed directory."""
    abs_path = os.path.abspath(path)
    if not abs_path.startswith(ALLOWED_ROOT):
        return f"Error: Access denied. Path must be within {ALLOWED_ROOT}"
    # ... proceed with reading
```

3. **Command allowlisting**: Don't let the agent run arbitrary commands in production:

```{python}
#| label: command-safety
ALLOWED_COMMANDS = ["python", "pytest", "git", "ls", "cat", "grep"]

def safe_run_command(command: str) -> str:
    """Only allow pre-approved commands."""
    first_word = command.split()[0]
    if first_word not in ALLOWED_COMMANDS:
        return f"Error: Command '{first_word}' is not allowed."
    # ... proceed with execution
```

4. **Token/cost limits**: Set maximum tokens per request and total spend limits when using APIs.

5. **Human-in-the-loop**: For destructive actions (file deletion, git push), always ask for confirmation.

---

# Next Steps

You now know how to:

- Evaluate and choose open-weight coding models
- Set up local inference with Ollama
- Build a coding agent from scratch
- Use production-ready tools like Aider and SWE-agent
- Switch between local and API-based models

## Where to Go From Here

- **Fine-tune a model** on your organization's codebase for better domain-specific results
- **Add RAG** (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) to give the agent access to documentation
- **Integrate with your IDE** using the Language Server Protocol (LSP)
- **Deploy internally** using tools like [vLLM](https://docs.vllm.ai/) or [TGI](https://huggingface.co/docs/text-generation-inference/) for team-wide access
- **Evaluate rigorously** using SWE-bench to track model improvements over time

::: {.callout-note}
## Further Reading

- [Aider Leaderboard](https://aider.chat/docs/leaderboards/) — Which models work best with Aider
- [SWE-bench Results](https://www.swebench.com) — Agent benchmark results
- [Open LLM Leaderboard](https://huggingface.co/spaces/open-llm-leaderboard/open_llm_leaderboard) — General model comparisons
- [Ollama Model Library](https://ollama.com/library) — Available local models
- [Hugging Face Code Models](https://huggingface.co/models?pipeline_tag=text-generation&sort=trending&search=code) — Browse coding models
:::

---

*This guide was created as part of a PhD research project at the University of Oslo. Last updated: March 2026.*
